current_flagged_state: All 7 landing sections use scroll-triggered fade-in-up: an IntersectionObserver watches each section, toggles a class that animates opacity 0->1 and translateY(~20-40px)->0, with staggered delays per child element. This is flagged as the strongest AI-tell on the site because it is applied uniformly to every section regardless of content, uses the exact library-default easing/duration/offset values seen across countless AI-generated and template sites (e.g. AOS, Framer templates), and telegraphs 'a component was dropped in' rather than a considered decision about what deserves motion.
goal: Replace the blanket per-section reveal with a motion budget that is small, deliberate, and legible as a choice: most content should simply be present (no entrance animation), any motion that remains should be concentrated where it earns attention (typically the hero, or one storytelling/data moment), and the underlying implementation should look like it was hand-considered (native CSS scroll-timeline or a single bespoke reveal) rather than a copy-pasted IntersectionObserver utility applied identically 7 times.

options.option: No entrance motion / content-first instant rendering
options.description: Remove scroll-triggered reveals entirely. Sections render fully visible on load with no opacity/transform choreography tied to viewport entry. Any motion is reserved for genuine state changes (hover, focus, data loading, form feedback), not for 'section appears as user scrolls to it'.
options.why_it_reads_human: A person who trusts their content doesn't need to stage its arrival. Instant, complete rendering signals confidence and respects the reader's time; it is also the opposite of the templated 'everything fades up' signature, because there is nothing left to template.
adoption_examples: Most content on stripe.com below the interactive hero widgets renders statically
A number of 2025-2026 'anti-AI-slop' redesign case studies explicitly cite removing all scroll reveals as the fix

options.description: Spend the entire motion budget on one deliberate, bespoke animation in the hero (e.g. a clip-path/mask text reveal, a marigold underline draw-on, a subtle counting stat, or a one-time entrance for the hero illustration/headline) and leave every section below it static.
options.why_it_reads_human: Mirrors how premium product sites actually behave: motion is treated as expensive and rationed to the single most important moment (first impression), not distributed evenly down the page. The asymmetry itself (motion up top, none below) reads as intentional editorial pacing rather than a library default running on every `<section>`.
adoption_examples: Linear.app - live in-product hero animation, restraint everywhere below the fold
Vercel.com hero grid/gradient treatment, static feature sections beneath it

options.description: Replace the IntersectionObserver + class-toggle JS entirely with native CSS: `animation-timeline: view()` and `animation-range` tie an @keyframes animation directly to scroll progress through the viewport, no JavaScript needed. Use sparingly (one or two elements, not all 7 sections) and vary the effect (not the same fade-in-up keyframes reused everywhere).
options.why_it_reads_human: Removing the JS observer plumbing in favor of a five-line CSS declaration is itself a signal of technical intentionality (someone chose the modern platform primitive), and because it's cheap to make each instance different, it discourages copy-pasting the identical effect across every section - the discipline of the tool nudges away from the tell it's replacing.

options.description: Drop scroll-position as the animation trigger altogether. Motion is reserved for things that actually change state: a button press, an accordion opening, a form validating, a filter updating a list, a nav condensing on scroll direction. No element animates purely because it entered the viewport.
options.why_it_reads_human: Motion that corresponds 1:1 with a user action or a real state transition reads as functional UI craftsmanship. Motion that fires because a threshold was crossed while scrolling reads as decoration bolted onto static content - the classic AI-slop signature callout is 'the same generic fade-in on every element,' and this option has no such thing to fade in.
adoption_examples: GOV.UK and other restraint-first government/enterprise sites

options.option: Sparse scroll-linked storytelling moment (one parallax/progress/counter element)
options.description: Keep exactly one scroll-linked effect somewhere in the middle of the page where it does real narrative work - e.g. a stat that counts up, a progress indicator tied to a multi-step process section, or a subtle background parallax on a single divider - and leave all other sections untouched.
options.why_it_reads_human: A single, well-placed scroll-linked moment shows the motion was authored for that specific piece of content (it explains or emphasizes something), rather than being a generic utility class draped over every `<section>` tag.
adoption_examples: Apple product pages (used far more selectively than commonly assumed)
Editorial long-form journalism scrollytelling (NYT/Pudding-style, used once per story arc, not per section)

recommended: Combine options 1 and 2: default to no entrance motion for the 6 non-hero sections (instant, fully-rendered content-first), and spend the site's entire motion budget on one bespoke hero moment - concretely, a clip-path/mask reveal or marigold underline draw-on for the hero headline using a plain CSS @keyframes animation on page load (not scroll-triggered), paired with a `prefers-reduced-motion` guard. If a dashboard-leaning stakeholder wants more perceived motion, add option 3 (CSS `animation-timeline: view()`) to exactly one below-the-fold element (e.g. a stats/credibility section with a counting number or a progress-style divider) rather than reviving fade-in-up everywhere - this keeps the JS surface at zero (satisfying the CSP script-src 'self' constraint trivially, since it needs no script at all) while still reading as a deliberate, single authored choice rather than a templated utility. This pairing directly targets the flagged problem (uniform fade-in-up on all 7 sections) by making the amount and placement of motion itself asymmetric and considered, which is what separates 'designed' from 'templated' in 2025-2026 site critiques.
trend_context: By mid-2026 the industry has explicitly named 'the same generic fade-in on every element' as one of the clearest AI-slop/template tells, alongside missing or absent motion entirely - the fix is not zero animation but restraint plus intentionality (does the motion communicate function, direct focus, or reinforce brand character?). Two parallel shifts are converging: (1) a performance/content-first backlash where 2026 sites strip decorative motion in favor of instant, readable content (typography and whitespace doing the work editorial sites used to reserve for animation), and (2) a platform shift where CSS scroll-driven animations (`animation-timeline: view()`, ~82%+ browser support mid-2026, Firefox behind a flag but shipping) are replacing IntersectionObserver-based JS entirely, run on the compositor thread, and are cheap enough that developers vary them per-instance rather than reusing one utility everywhere. Benchmark 'humanist tech' sites (Linear, Vercel, Stripe, Arc, Attio) concentrate motion almost entirely in the hero (often a live product clip or shader/gradient treatment) and are markedly restrained - flat, static - through the rest of the page, which is the opposite distribution from the uniform-fade-everywhere pattern this site currently ships.
vanilla_css_notes: 1) Delete the IntersectionObserver + class-toggle script and any 'reveal on scroll' utility applied to the 6 non-hero sections; those sections should have no opacity/transform styling tied to scroll at all - they are simply present in normal flow. 2) For the hero-only moment, use a plain @keyframes triggered on load (no scroll dependency), e.g. `@keyframes reveal { from { clip-path: inset(0 100% 0 0); } to { clip-path: inset(0 0 0 0); } }` applied once via `animation: reveal 700ms cubic-bezier(.2,.7,.2,1) both;` - this needs zero JavaScript. 3) If adding the one scroll-linked element (option 3), use native CSS only: give the target a `view-timeline-name: --stat-reveal;` (or rely on the shorthand `animation-timeline: view();` on the animated element itself), then `animation: count-up 1s linear both; animation-timeline: view(); animation-range: entry 10% cover 40%;` - wrap in `@supports (animation-timeline: view())` so browsers without support (older Firefox) simply show the static end-state with no animation (progressive enhancement, not a broken/invisible state). 4) Important CSP constraint: since `script-src 'self'` forbids inline `<script>` and inline event handlers, any JS that remains (e.g. a `requestAnimationFrame`-driven number counter if you want that instead of CSS `@property`/counter tricks) must live in an external file loaded via `<script src="/js/hero.js" defer></script>` - never inline. If you want a pure-CSS animated counter, use `@property --num { syntax: '<integer>'; inherits: false; initial-value: 0; }` with a CSS custom property transition, which needs no script at all. 5) Never set base opacity to 0 in a stylesheet without an unconditional fallback - if the JS/CSS-timeline path fails to apply for any reason, content must not be permanently invisible; prefer `animation-fill-mode: both` on the keyframes themselves rather than a separate 'hidden until revealed' class.
accessibility_notes: Wrap all remaining motion (the hero reveal and any scroll-linked element) in `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) { animation: none !important; }` (or set `animation-duration: 0.01ms` and rely on end-state via `animation-fill-mode`) so users who've opted out get instant, fully-visible content - this satisfies WCAG 2.3.3 (Animation from Interactions, AAA) and the broader 2.2.2 Pause/Stop/Hide expectation for motion that plays automatically. Because the recommended pattern renders content in normal flow without JS-dependent visibility toggling, there is no risk of content being trapped at opacity:0 for no-JS users or screen readers that don't fire scroll/intersection events - a real accessibility bug the current IntersectionObserver approach risks if the script fails to load. Ensure the hero reveal doesn't shift focus order or delay when interactive elements (nav, CTA) become operable - animate visual properties (opacity/clip-path/transform) only, never the presence of the element in the DOM or its tab order.
pitfalls: A hero-only reveal can still look templated if it's the exact same fade+translateY keyframes just moved to one element - it must be a genuinely distinct technique (mask/clip-path reveal, underline draw, or a subtle scale) to read as authored rather than relocated. Native CSS `animation-timeline: view()` is a new hammer, and the same mistake (blanket application to all 7 sections with identical `animation-range` values) reproduces the exact tell being fixed, just via newer syntax - it should be used on at most one element, not as a wholesale IntersectionObserver replacement across the page. Going to zero motion everywhere (pure option 1, no hero moment at all) risks reading as unfinished or static to a stakeholder who explicitly wants some 'dashboard-leaning' polish, so total motion removal without the compensating hero moment may cause pushback even though it is the more defensible design choice. Scroll-linked parallax/counters (option 5) date quickly and can feel gimmicky if the number/stat doesn't carry real narrative weight - only use it if there is an honest stat worth counting up to, not decoration for its own sake.
